name: Sulfur
name_origin: From the Latin 'sulpur' or 'sulphur', possibly of Sanskrit or Arabic origin meaning 'burning stone'. Known since prehistoric times as 'brimstone'.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Sulfur is the tenth most abundant element by mass in the universe and the fifth most abundant on Earth. It occurs as the native element near volcanoes and hot springs, and in many sulfide and sulfate minerals.
states: hydrogen sulfide in natural gas and crude oil
amino acids cysteine and methionine in proteins

compounds.name: Sulfuric acid
compounds.summary: One of the most important industrial chemicals; produced in greater volumes than any other and used in fertilizer manufacture, batteries, and metal processing.

compounds.name: Hydrogen sulfide
compounds.summary: A toxic, flammable gas with a rotten-egg odor; a major byproduct of petroleum refining and a building block of sulfur recovery.

compounds.name: Sulfur dioxide
compounds.summary: A colorless gas and major air pollutant; used as a food preservative, bleaching agent, and in sulfuric acid production.

compounds.name: Sulfur trioxide
compounds.summary: An intermediate in the contact process for making sulfuric acid; reacts violently with water.

compounds.name: Pyrite
compounds.summary: Iron disulfide ('fool's gold'); historically a source of sulfur and a sulfuric acid feedstock.

compounds.name: Gypsum
compounds.summary: Hydrated calcium sulfate; used in plaster of Paris, drywall, and as a soil amendment.

uses: Production of sulfuric acid (predominant use, vital for fertilizer manufacture)
Vulcanization of rubber to improve elasticity and durability
Preservative and antioxidant in dried fruits and wines (as sulfites)
reactivity_summary: Sulfur is a moderately reactive nonmetal that exists in many allotropes; the stable form at room temperature is rhombic S8 rings. It burns in air with a blue flame to form sulfur dioxide and combines directly with most metals and many nonmetals to form sulfides and sulfates.
isotopes_summary: Sulfur has four stable isotopes: S-32 (~95.0%), S-33 (~0.75%), S-34 (~4.25%), and S-36 (~0.01%). S-35, with a half-life of about 87 days, is widely used as a radioactive tracer in biochemistry.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Sulfur
